data.title: Chart Of The Day: Why I'm Watching This Subtle Market Shift
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It’s subtle. But it’s there. I’m talking about the modest rotation out of more offensive sectors and in to more defensive ones.

Look at these two MoneyShow Charts of the Day. The first shows the relative performance of the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) versus the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF). The second shows the same ratio comparison between the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Chart 1: Health Care Vs. Financials

(Source: StockCharts.com)

Chart 2: Consumer Staples Vs. Consumer Discretionary

(Source: StockCharts.com)

What stands out? Health care stocks are “breaking out” relative to financials after a long period of underperformance. You’re seeing the same thing for staples stocks versus discretionary ones, although in that case it’s a more muted move.

While I didn’t include a chart here, I’d also note that the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) has returned roughly 7.5% in the last three months. Bond prices move in the opposite direction of bond yields, so that’s being accompanied by a sharp drop in the latter. The yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury Note just sank below 4% briefly, for instance. That was its lowest level since April.

Add it all up and I’d say markets are increasingly betting on economic weakness. Again, it’s subtle. It’s early. But it’s worth watching. Because if this trend were to continue, I’d probably dial back my “Be Bold” stance on markets.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Are We In A Bubble?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tesla headlines earnings this week (0:25). GM pops, Netflix slides (1:50). Intel's strong results (5:50). Beyond Meat's meme stock moment (7:00). CPI report, rate cuts (8:35). Interesting AI earnings coming next week (10:25).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha, always great to talk to you on these Wall Street Roundups. Welcome back to another week.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

RS: We've got a government shutdown that still exists. We've got some tariff news with Canada. We've got major earnings. Where should we start this week?

BS: It's funny. I kinda forgot about the government shutdown. It's so pushed to the background.

I think this week has all been about earnings getting revved up. Tesla (TSLA) obviously, the headliner just in terms of the profile the company has, though the earnings themselves were neither here nor there.

The stock was up about 2% after earnings. It missed on its bottom line, and its margin shrank, but revenue was up 12% and beat expectations.

The company warned that it's facing near term uncertainty with shifting trade, tariff and fiscal policy, though I think we all knew that.

So that wasn't really new information, though slightly noteworthy that the company felt the need to to acknowledge that.

I think in general, people took the Tesla report as fine, a step on the way of the comeback that started after Musk returned to full time duties after the DOGE experiment.

So I think investors are basically in a wait and see mode. The stock has already recovered quite a bit from what it lost during the DOGE situation.

And so I think really they're just looking to the future and and I think deliveries are gonna matter and production's gonna matter as those reports come out in the coming months.

RS: And there's many more companies to get into when it comes to earnings. What else would you put on that list?

BS: As long as we're talking EVs, (GM) is an interesting one.

It popped 15% after its earnings. It beat expectations, raised guidance. The fundamental lesson from that was that the tariff impact was not as bad as the company expected. Meanwhile, GM has been increasing its market share.

It reached its highest market share since 2017. So the company's executing well. There's worries of an EV demand softening as people went out and bought EVs as the tax credit was expiring.

And so there's there's a worry, and this is something that could impact Tesla as well. There's a worry that this sort of packed the channel in that regard. But overall, investors were very upbeat about GM's report. And it just goes to show that the car industry is more than just Tesla.

And then on the downside, the big loser, at least among the headline stocks, was Netflix (NFLX).

It dropped 10% after its earnings. Revenue rose 17% from last year, beating expectations where the company took a 600,000,000 charge related to an ongoing dispute with Brazilian tax authorities. It also saw some margin decline.

I think that margin decline is probably more of a worry than the Brazilian tax situation which will resolve itself eventually. It's kind of a near term hurdle, but not really an existential threat to the company.

I think investors are probably a little bit more worried about margins shrinking a little bit.

RS: We had Hardika Singh on from Fundstrat on Investing Experts this past week on Sunday. And she was talking about how there's such an opportunity to be buying the dip these days as the stock market moves in, let's say, unprecedented ways and things that are sometimes hard to understand, but the opportunities are there.

And as you mentioned Netflix, Cathie Wood, she added, she bought the dip on Netflix. Anything to say there or what you're seeing from market participants or anything to note about Cathie's position or any other large positions that are being taken there or anything else to note with Netflix?

BS: I think you're getting at the fundamental debate that's going on in the market right now. I think a lot of people are worried that the market as a whole and the AI stocks specifically are gonna turn into a pumpkin sometime soon.

It's gonna strike midnight, and we're all gonna find ourselves running without shoes on, if that metaphor stretches that far. I mean, Cathie Wood is a super bull, always has been.

So I don't know, she's looking long term, right? I mean, her idea is that these stocks are going to sort of change the fundamental way in which we do business and live our lives.

And so I don't know how much she's factoring in near term factors like tariffs and things like that. So I think that goes to the heart of the debate, to what your horizon is.

I mean, if you have a 10 horizon, certainly there's opportunities to get stocks pretty cheaply. Now, if you have a much more near term, if you're more of a trader, there are a lot of worries out there.

And so that's the I think the question that's being asked is when a stock like Netflix drops 10%, is that the start of a bubble bursting or is that the best opportunity you're going to get for the next foreseeable future to buy that stock?

RS: And to the point of long term investing in that space, Dan Rayburn, an expert in the media and streaming space, he writes and speaks a lot outside of Seeking Alpha. We've had him on a few times on Investing Experts. And the last episode he was on, we called it Netflix is Still King. He espouses a bullish long term perspective on that.

We saw results from Intel (INTC) on Thursday. What would you say about that? Tech obviously top of mind for many investors as it has such a large effect on the rest of the market.

BS: Last check today, the company reported last night, stock is up about 2%.

It had strong results and guidance. People see this as signs that the turnaround at Intel is proceeding a pace. There are still worries about valuation and worries about execution.

The stock has rallied significantly recently. It's up 57% since NVIDIA (NVDA) announced its investment in mid September. So it's had a huge upswing over the past few weeks, a very short period of time.

And so now I think it's a matter of investors in a show me kind of mood. So we'll see in the coming quarters whether Intel can turn what seems to be a switch in momentum for the company into real bottom line results.

RS: Any other stock specific highlights that you would share with listeners?

BS: Just to straying off of earnings a little bit, I think it's worth it as we talk about the debate around whether or not we're in a bubble.

Beyond Meat (BYND) had a a meme stock moment this past week. So it jumped from 52¢ at the close of last Thursday to $3.62 on Tuesday. It hit $7.69 along the way.

There was some news to kind of sprinkle in there and drive the stock. It had an expanded deal with Walmart (WMT), but this was mostly a short squeeze meme stock situation, and there were sizable gains.

I mean, nothing in the the hundreds of percentage points that you saw from Beyond Meat, but Krispy Kreme (DNUT), GoPro (GPRO), iRobot (IRBT), also substantial. And these were all sort of the cluster of meme stocks that have emerged in 2025.

So I think if you're skeptical about the market in general, I think situations like this raise a red flag when you see frothy situations like that.

If your argument is that we're in a bubble, then these kind of situations pop up and it shows that there's there's kind of a a lack of rationality in the market.

I think a bull would just say we've had meme stocks going on through the twenty twenties. It's just part of the market at this point. Every once in a while, this stuff is gonna bubble up, and you can't really make longer term decisions based on how often this is happening.

RS: Hardika had another point in that episode about the meme stock ETF (MEME) that it just recently came back on board. So I think reflective of that point for sure.

I mentioned the government shutdown at the beginning. You said you weren't very focused on it, but we have had a dearth of data as we've talked about because of that shutdown.

We did get the CPI report this week. Some bullishness if you wanna dive into that pool. What would you say about that report or economic data in general these days?

BS: That's a good point about the economic data, especially we're theoretically two weeks away from getting the next jobs report.

Pretty skeptical that's gonna happen. I think there were headlines today that the next CPI report is in danger as well. So this might be the last CPI that we get for some period of time.

But pretty much in line with what people were expecting, the inflation rate, the core CPI was 3%, which is where we sort of settled.

Officially, the Fed is still targeting a 2% inflation rate, but I think there's sort of an understanding that 3% is fine. You know, like, we wish we had 2%, but we'll settle for 3%.

So the biggest thing in the economic calendar next week is the Fed decision. Everyone pretty much expects the Fed to cut rates by 25 basis points. That's pretty much baked in 97% chance if you look at the trading so far.

Also, another rate cut is expected for December. It's roughly fifty fifty if there's gonna be a third rate cut in January. So people are expecting the Fed to take these stair steps lower in the next several meetings.

And so as long as inflation doesn't spike anywhere beyond sort of that 3% range, I think that rate cutting situation is going to unfold as the market expects.

RS: We've got some major earnings coming next week to continue with the crux of earning season. What would you highlight for investors?

BS: I think an interesting dichotomy to look at is Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta (META), both AI plays, both coming out next week.

So its last earnings reports, it spiked 11% in the day after the earnings, but has since drifted off that level. So it's down about 5% from that post earnings jump.

Basically, there's a valuation worry at Meta. There's also a spending concern. It's sort of a habit at Meta to push a lot of chips in the middle when it sees an idea that happened with the metaverse and now it's happening with AI.

And so the question is whether these bets are going to to pay off. I mean, the core business, the Facebook business, the social media business has been extremely strong.

And the question is, did the cash that it's investing from these businesses into the new businesses, are those eventually going to fall to the bottom line?

And so that's gonna be what people have their eye on in Meta. So there was like a a rush of enthusiasm after the last earnings report, which was extremely strong, and then a caution that set in afterwards.

Meanwhile, Google, after it released its last earnings report, which is also extremely strong, it largely received a shrug.

The stock didn't move much at all after that earnings report. However, the stock has been making its way higher ever since. So it's up about 36% since that last earnings report.

So the opposite reaction to Meta where there was sort of a indifference to the the actual earnings report, but then kind of a drift up as enthusiasm grew over the past several months.

So it'll be interesting to see when Google's report comes out, when Alphabet's report comes out, whether or not the results can justify the enthusiasm that's now been priced in.

RS: Anything else to add earnings wise or next week wise?

BS: We've got Apple (AAPL) coming out. The new iPhone came out, so there there might be some commentary on that.

Though I think probably the next quarter will be the key one for that. Another interesting thing about Apple is Apple is up only about 5% this year and only turned positive for the year in September.

It's another stock that slipped a little after its last earnings report in July and it's up about 30% since then. So like Alphabet, it's sort of seen a growing enthusiasm over the past several months.

In Apple's case, part of the upside that it has seen is that it made a $100,000,000,000 manufacturing commitment to the US, which was seen as a hedge against the tariff situation.

So Apple's pretty exposed to the trade tensions between China and the US, So that $100,000,000,000 commitment is seen as a way to get an exemption if in fact those tariffs really bite down on the company.

It saw a boost in the August, September time frame as that worry, the tariff worry, seemed to get relieved.

And then Microsoft (MSFT) is also reporting next week, a company that also is an AI play. Look at the Azure numbers as that come out, the cloud numbers.

It saw 26% growth in its cloud business last quarter, which was up from 21% in the previous quarter and 20% the year before. So it stepped up to a higher growth plane with that last quarter. I think the question is whether it's going to continue to see that higher growth rate or whether it's gonna retreat back to where it was before.

data.title: Pagaya: The Foundation Is Set For A Long-Term Rerating
data.description:
Introduction

I earlier covered technology-driven Chinese lending businesses like Finvolution (FINV) and QFIN Holdings (QFIN). What stood out for these businesses was their business model, which uses AI for inclusion of borrowers that were rejected by the traditional banking system. One such business operating in the US is Pagaya (NASDAQ:PGY). While not exactly the same business model, PGY too uses technology to serve the borrowers that are rejected by the traditional banking system.

While this segment is not served by the traditional banking system due to its high-risk nature, PGY is trying to solve this problem with the help of advanced AI technology to screen creditworthy borrowers among this underserved category. This leaves PGY with a large unserved market and Total Addressable Market (TAM).

Recently the stock has started to turn profitable after many years of investment and building trust among its partners. Now it seems like PGY is going through a pivotal moment where operational leverage would kick in, leading to fast bottom-line growth. Additionally, PGY's valuation also seems to be decent to me, especially considering the ramp-up in profitability going forward. Therefore, I am issuing a buy rating on the stock.

Company overview

You can compare PGY to a scrap collector in the lending industry. When a loan application is rejected by one of the lending partners of PGY it is then sent to PGY for further screening. PGY screens these loan applications through its proprietary AI and machine learning technology and pulls out potential creditworthy loan applicants that might have been rejected by the traditional screening process of lending partners. Once screened, PGY sends a loan proposal to the partner lender, and then the lender sends it to the borrowers. Once the loan proposal is accepted, it creates a temporary asset facilitated by PGY, also known as network volume.

PGY keeps its balance sheet light by funding these loans either through a financing vehicle or securitizing these loans into asset-backed securities (ABS) or through a pre-funded model where it raises capital from institutional investors before assets are created. This process makes PGY a pure-play technology company that facilitates asset creation (providing value to borrowers and lenders) and securitization (providing value to investors) and thus generates a commission for itself, which it terms as "take rate."

A Turn to Profitability

After many quarters of being in GAAP losses, PGY has finally turned into GAAP profits starting from the first quarter of 2025.

The company has been posting consistent growth in network volume growth over the last many quarters. However, there is a subtle shift in the last few quarters, with the company now shifting toward other segments as they have solidified their position in the personal loan category. The share of auto and POS loans has been rising over the last few quarters. The share has risen from 9% a year ago to ~30% now. Furthermore, management plans to take this share to 1/3rd each for all three loan categories, cumulatively generating $25 billion in annual network volume from the current run rate of $10.5-11.5 billion. This looks to be quite a good strategy, as the company could leverage its existing relationship with partners to penetrate into other loan categories to pursue growth.

With respect to profitability, PGY measures its operational efficiency through a non-GAAP financial metric that they call Fee Revenue Less Production Costs (FRLPC). FLPC is calculated by subtracting production costs from fee revenue, which is compensation to lending partners for acquiring and originating assets. The company reported a near-record-high FRLPC margin (~4.8%) in the most recent quarter, driven by improved monetization in the personnel loan category, which is starting to mature, leading to a better take rate. The improvement on the FRLPC front is having a leveraging effect on the bottom line of the company, leading to the turn into profitability.

Outlook

I believe PGY's recent turnaround has legs, and it should extend in years to come as the company would now reap the benefits of years of brand building and investments. The company has 31 lending partners with a 100% retention rate, including names like US Bank, SoFi, Ally, and Klarna. On the investment side the company has an ABS investor base of ~145 entities.

As PGY has established itself as a trusted partner for both investors and lending partners, I believe it would be easier now to partner with more and bigger partners. As per management, they are targeting to add at least 2-4 lending partners per year with ongoing discussion with 80% of the top 25 banks in the US.

Having multiple partners is important, as it will give PGY access to a larger pool of potential borrowers to source from. This not only will increase PGY's network volume but also increase the data points for PGY, which it could later use to further train its AI and machine learning models.

As per PGY's management, they see a combined $850 billion worth of serviceable addressable market within the personal, auto, and POS loan categories. This in itself is quite a huge market; furthermore, the company can also leverage its relationship with existing partners to further expand into other loan categories, just like it is doing in the POS and auto segments. And frankly, they had done quite a commendable job in the POS and auto expansion by growing the share from 9% to 30% within a year. This shows how PGY can leverage its existing relation and its own goodwill to expand into other loan categories. So for the network volume, I believe PGY has a huge headroom to grow for at least the next 5 to 10 years given the huge end market.

In addition to good network volume growth and subsequently topline growth, I believe PGY's bottom line growth would not only grow but grow at a much faster pace thanks to the operating leverage. As mentioned earlier, PGY has earlier invested quite a sum in itself to build all the necessary infrastructure, partnerships, and goodwill in order to grow.

Now, as everything now seems to be in place, PGY would need increasingly less expenditure to grow in its huge TAM. If we look at recent quarters, core Opex as a percentage of FRLPC has consistently trended downward, signaling operational leverage at play. I believe this leverage should continue to benefit the company in the future as well, leading to faster bottom-line growth.

What to expect from 3Q25

PGY is set to announce its 3Q25 results on the 10th of November. I believe current momentum should persist in the upcoming quarter report, and it would be obvious to expect another quarter with GAAP profits.

I would be keeping an eye on the network volume growth, as to how fast it grows; as per management, they are expecting a network volume of $2.75 billion to $2.95 billion. Any kind of positive surprise on this metric would be taken very positively by the street.

Another important metric that I would be looking for would be the FLRPC margin. While this margin is near the upper end of management's long-term guidance range. I believe the possibility of this margin overshooting the long-term guidance of 4-5% is quite high, as the recent ramp-up in auto and POS becomes more profitable in the coming quarter after a less profitable ramp-up period. So I would be expecting a surprise on this front either in the upcoming quarter or in quarters after that.

Lastly, while the delinquency ratio has remained stable for the last many quarters, I would be keeping an eye on this given the recent asset quality concerns across the banking industry that surfaced during the ongoing results season.

Risk

PGY's business model revolves around sourcing creditworthy borrowers from a pool of potential borrowers. Sourcing creditworthy borrowers is a key value proposition of PGY, so if there are situations where PGY's sourced loans become bad more frequently, i.e., the delinquency rate rises, then this could be a problem for the entire business model of PGY.

Partners that provide investments to buy out these packaged loans sourced by PGY might lose trust in PGY's ability to source high-quality loans, leading to lower demand for its product.

Fortunately, PGY's credit quality is quite robust, and delinquency ratios in personal and auto loans are either stable or improving. Moreover, the investing partner base is consistently growing.

Is PGY worth buying now

I prefer to value a lending business based on its price-to-book ratio in conjunction with ROE. The price-to-book ratio tells how much premium the market is willing to give to a business, and ROE tells how efficiently a business is utilizing its book value to generate return.

For PGY, its current forward price-to-book ratio is trending near 4.48x, which indicates that the market is assigning a hefty premium for PGY's business. Meanwhile, if we look at the ROE, it currently is negative given PGY's losses in the earlier quarter; however, since PGY is turning around, it should report a significant surge in profits in the next few quarters. If we use the consensus EPS estimate of $2.77 for 2025, we would arrive at 38% forward ROE (ROE calculated by EPS/book value per share).

Based on the 38% ROE and 4.48x P/B ratio, one could arrive at a yield ratio (ROE/price to book) (a measure to value a company based on market premium and capital efficiency) of 8.5%.

If we compare the yield ratio of PGY with its peers I belive PGY is decently placed. In PGY's case, I believe its business model deserves a premium, given that it is developing a moat-like structure as a trusted screener and consolidator of loans. Furthermore, I believe PGY's ROE has good upside potential given it has just started to turn around, and operating leverage would make it produce more return on lesser incremental capital, leading to a potential expansion in ROE and yield ratio going forward.

Company Ticker P/B (TTM) BVPS EPS Est. 2025 2025 ROE Yield ratio
Pagaya Technologies PGY 4.57 7.13 2.77 38.88% 8.51%
LendingTree TREE 6.06 8.89 4.31 48.48% 8.00%
LendingClub LC 1.41 13.02 1.14 8.76% 6.21%
Chime Financial CHYM 2.96 3.89 -4.24 -109.08% -36.85%
OneMain Holdings OMF 1.96 28.84 6.36 22.05% 11.25%

Given decent valuations and a potential of ROE expansion going forward, I believe PGY is a good buy.

data.title: 2008 Vs. 2025: 3 Similarities, And 3 Ways To Avoid The Same Fate
data.description:
Once again, I'm here not to tell anyone what to do with their money. My job here is to share my experience, including decades of research into a wide range of investment strategies and vehicles. Prior to the emergence of ETFs, I analyzed mutual funds for my then-advisory clients, did a little stock investing, and on 3 separate occasions managed a mutual fund.

The first one started on August 14, 2008. What happened next? This:

That's a 46% decline in under 7 months. Not because of anything that investors did not know about in 2007, or even as far back at 2006. Housing market woes, debt build up, overconfidence on the part of investors, etc.

The fund I managed was of the "alternative" variety. I used mutual funds and ETFs, and allocated to them in an approach that was essentially a liquid version of a hedge fund of funds. I used the very first covered call mutual funds and closed-end funds back then. I've written briefly about those here before, those predecessors of the popular and ubiquitous ones of today, a market now dominated by names like JP Morgan Asset Management, NEOS and Yield Max.

Ads like this are seemingly everywhere now. They offer big time income rates.

Other features? Who cares! Well, I care. This article is not mainly about covered call ETFs. I've said my piece on that, and responded to several hundred comments over the years here. So there's not much new for me to say on that, other than the perception problems still exist.

I have been told by some commenters that investors in alternative ETFs, be they covered call or other types, are well aware that they do not hold up well when the market falls. I'm with them there, since we just saw this earlier this year.

That's a 6-month snapshot, just a moment in time, but one that reminds us that we now have a virtually unlimited number of ways to invest in and around the S&P 500 index. And frankly, many of them do not hold up to sustained scrutiny. Oh, they will work when the market recovers as fast as it fell, which is all we've seen since 2008. At some point, the drop will be followed by another drop, and another, and then finally, a period of volatility but no net movement in the major indexes.

I don't have a date when that will occur, but I'm quite prepared for when it does. Even it happens to be the day you read this article. Because managing risk at times like this is not about giving up gobs of upside. It is about HOW we proactively avoid big losses.

How to proactively avoid big losses (2025-26 version)

As you recall, 2008 was the year I became a mutual fund manager for the first time. I'm glad to say that I survived that well enough to get a similar gig some years later, before finally retiring from the business of running other people's money.

As I recall, my fund lost about 16% when the market was nearly cut in half. All I thought about then was the absolute return. So I was not accepting any pats on the back for beating the market by 30% in 7 months. My job was to manage risk. And relatively speaking, I did. But having $84 for every $100 I started with was no solace. Not to the child of a Dad who grew up during the Great Depression in NYC.

If I had been managing a "hedge fund," since most of them blew up around us back then, perhaps my performance would have been lauded, and we would have attracted capital. "Those guys who didn't lose half their fund or more." No such thing, ever. Money chases performance, then chases something else. But not relative return. It will be the same way this time around. Which is not good news for alternative ETFs that lose 80% or 90% of what the S&P 500 does. There will be a lot of them.

Why tell those old war stories? To remind us that a few things about 2025 are very different from back in 2008, when I received my "initiation" to the managed fund business. Most importantly, that the whole idea of "diversification" does not work when the market correlates. And now, more than ever, is what happens when the stock market sells off.

Above, I've gone way back to the proverbial well, to recall some of the things I hoped would plug the big hole in the boat of any portfolio I was associated with. "None of the above" was the best answer, as you can see.

The market started to recover at this point. But here's the issue. 2008 was a case of excesses built up over many years, some even stemming from the dot-com bubble that popped in 2000, and led to 3 straight down years for the S&P 500, the only time in history that has occurred.

Did anyone count on that? NO. Did anyone have a chance to proactively prepare for it? YES. Did they? Well, I can only account for one who did (me), much to the ridicule and/or distain of many who knew me. That included some clients, who felt like we were being too conservative with their assets.

My response: "you have enough money to live on for the rest of your life and at least a generation afterward ($2-5mm in investable assets was our client sweet spot). Some left us for "greener pastures" in 2007 and early 2008. Others agreed with the "avoid big loss" approach and joined us.

That chart above shows that 2008 was toward the end of a longer, secular market cycle. I prefer to look at such S&P 500 cycles as follows: take the peak (in prices and extreme sentiment), track the decline to its low...and then track the recovery to the old high.

That last part is the key. If I have $100 and it drops to $50, we all know I need to make 100% gain to make up for the 50% loss I just endured. But when I make that 100% gain, which in the period shown above took "only" about 3 years, through late 2011, all I did was earn back what I had lost.

Bottom line on that previous chart: from March 2000 through December 2011, the return of the S&P 500, including dividends, was zero. Let that soak in.

This is not some red flag-waving moment. It is just a reminder that markets are not rational, in either direction. Was the S&P 500's trailing P/E Ratio excessive at 35-45x on the left side of this chart? How about after the spike in 2008, when it dropped to 14x during 2011? Markets overshoot in both directions. This is a great time to keep that in mind.

2008 is ancient history. Or is it?

I've tried to this point to lay out some of my most relevant, vivid memories from the last time "buy the dips" didn't work. I do not know when investors will again lament that it did not occur again. But I know what I think is similar to, and better than (for DIY investors) than 17 years ago.

So I'll finish by explaining those and then showing a short list of "go-to's" for me. They are all ETFs. As my regular readers know, I use options to manage risk as well. I don't use single stocks. Because single stocks do not work when the market is being driven by a scenario in which strong liquidity turns to tight liquidity.

That, and the speculation that fed into it, are what is the downfall of every major stock market cycle. It will be the downfall of this current one too. When? When it is, that's when!

In other words, I do not spend my days guessing about the future. I plan for disaster always. In the same way I did not wait to turn 60 years old to buy life insurance. I became a father for the first time at age 32. I had insurance when it became that much more important to have it. Portfolio disaster protection is generally similar, just a very different type of decision. You don't want to wait until everyone knows there's a problem.

The routes to preserving capital are still there ("go to cash" etc.) but the best "bang for the buck" (put options on S&P 500 or other major market indexes, some of the ETFs I'll show) are best considered proactively. Not when you are reading articles about them every day. (Side prediction: the ETFs I mention below will be front page news...when the S&P 500 is down 20-30% or more).

2025 vs. 2008: what's the same?

1. Margin debt is off the charts. Leverage pops the bubble.

2. Everyone is speculating. ODTE options, those covered call ETFs, and much more.

3. FOMO and wealth envy are everywhere. There can be an entire article about this subject. Books, even. But suffice it to say, this is part of what drives the "chase" for higher asset prices, which works until it fails. Just ask the "Big Short" era folks.

2025 vs. 2008: what's different?

1. Many more ETFs that manage risk without sacrificing all return. I used Seeking Alpha's nicely-upgraded and easy to use ETF Screener. I asked it to show me the current funds in 8 different alternative categories. There were 482 of them. How many existed 10 years ago? Only 70 of them. How many were available back before 2007? 8 of them, all single inverse or leveraged inverse ETFs.

A list of my go-to's, which I use tactically in all market conditions, follows here. Note that these are NOT buy and hold funds. I may hold them weeks to months at most. My whole process is about assessing the level of major risk at any point in time, and applying a set of "defensive assets" via a combination of these ETFs. This is part of what I call "playing offense and defense at the same time."

In that chart above, I've run 6-month rolling returns of 4 of many I consider. There are 2 volatility ETFs (one that is more sensitive as it uses short term VIX futures, the other less so as it spread its VIX exposure out a few months). There's also ProShares Short S&P 500 ETF (SH), which is one of the "originals" I've used since before 2008. And ProShares UltraPro Short QQQ (SQQQ), part of a newer and expanding crop of leveraged inverse ETFs.

In short (pardon the pun), inverse-leveraged funds need to be used in small position size, and for short time frames. But as I see it, they cover a lot of ground with a small capital commitment, similar to a degree as put options do.

2. The best T-bill rates in a long time. Here is the past 24-year history of 6-month US Treasury Bills. They are off their peak level, but I see one of the highest levels this century. As I see it, any time I can get nearly 4% annualized to check out the scene for a half a year with some of my capital, it does 2 things: it pays me to wait, but it also provides an anchor, allowing me to be more aggressive and tactical with some of my other capital. The return of the 2 parts of my portfolio is the blended return and risk profile I want to have at any point in time.

3. Better access to information...just separate the help from the hype.

The great news is that investing has gone "mainstream." The bad news is that while we've never had more data and information at our fingertips, I dare say we've never used less of it. But that's an individual decision.

The bottom-line, is yours

Use that information, and use it with a skeptical eye, always. Be a self-directed investor. That means learning, analyzing and training to make decisions. Not necessarily trading decisions, since this is ultimately about investment allocation. And risk management.

This article was intended to set the scene for why and how I manage risk. Its essentially what I do for a living. Just for my own family now, after decades of doing it for other families. But without a doubt, there are enough similarities to 2008, and other very tough market cycles to take note. And not simply to blow them off, but to account for not only the possibility of what can happen, but virtually any scenario.

Final note for now: nothing I presented above has to mean long-term returns suffer. Not at all. If the S&P 500 rises continuously for another 5 years, sure. But more likely, the capital saved from adding proactive defense to one's offense puts an investor in great shape to capitalize on volatile markets, no matter what the trend is.

data.title: QuantumScape: Buying Opportunities Present Again Amid New Milestones And Recent Selloff
data.description:
Just barely three months after my last coverage on QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS), its value has already soared by over 40%. As an investor in this speculative stock, adding my position even after getting close to $10 was indeed a right move. As its development phase continues with new milestones, the market seems to be seeing the value it may offer to the industry. And although it has not generated any revenue yet, QS is approaching more important phases. Considering its current fundamentals, QS still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operations. Valuation is also justifiable at this level, so it still has some upside. Technicals conform with them as the bullish trend persists despite some selling volume due to the recent overbuying.

I Believe It's Worth It

Whether newcomers or not, QuantumScape Corporation requires a lot of patience, trust, and optimism, balanced with timely information. As an investor here for nearly a year, I'm already satisfied with the gains I've generated. Yet, I believe that QS still has more upside potential despite being a speculative stock. It remains on track with its timeline and milestones, which shows that it has a high capacity to deliver what we expect from it. This is supported by its continued collaboration with Murata. Once everything is set, a demand influx is possible. Why? The uniqueness of oxide ceramic electrolytes may complement lithium metal anodes to support all functional values. Its expected stability and compatibility with lithium may attract battery companies to switch from sulfide to ceramic electrolytes. This is possible as I explained the downsides of using sulfide-type electrolytes in my previous article. And if this materializes, QS may capture the attention of other original equipment manufacturers or OEMs.

Last month, QS and Volkswagen's PowerCo debuted the first live demonstration of solid-state lithium batteries or SSLBs in Ducati Motorcycle. They used QSE-5 battery cells that were produced using the Cobra separator process. This was quite a breakthrough since it showed how the development of the first anode-free SSLB moved from laboratory phases to real-world applications. This gave us confidence that QS would be able to ship samples before 2025 ends.

At this point, QS is still within its target as it targets the need for batteries that excel in five areas, such as charging speed, cost-effectiveness, energy density, lifespan, and safety. In its Q3 2025 presentation, QS proved that it passed in charging speed as the battery life of QSE-5 B-samples only needed 12.2 minutes to increase from 10% to 80%. It also retained over 95% energy at over 1,000 full cycle equivalents.

What's even more interesting is that QS has already started shipping its QSE-5 B1 samples in line with its goals this year. As we all know, these samples are now the most advanced QS cells and have separators using Cobra processes. With that, QS is on its way to a high-volume commercial production for EVs and other applications. For the remainder of the year, we expect it to start installing higher-volume cell production equipment and expand its commercial engagements. By 2026, it may start its field testing in Ducati and expand commercial engagements. These are highly attainable at this point.

But at this point, what's really important is that QS can still cover all expenses related to its development phases. This will ensure that it can deliver on time without compromising quality and accuracy. In its most recent quarter, its R&D expenses amounted to $92.1M, down by 5.1% YoY from $97.0M in Q3 2024. Meanwhile, its general and administrative expenses only amounted to $22.9M versus $33.2M YoY. This should not be surprising since QS has already advanced far enough in its development. After the successful integration of Cobra into its baseline cell production, QS enhanced its efficiency and managed expenses better. QS assures investors of its sustainability with its decent cash runway. On a monthly basis, it incurred an average amount of $30.6M in R&D expenses.

For its non-operating income and expenses, things still seemed manageable for QS. Interest income decreased, which may be attributed mainly due to the the series of interest rate cut. But it still helped to reduce the operating loss. As a result, it incurred a net loss of -$105.8M, up by 11.5% from -$119.7M YoY. Its EPS was -$0.18 but better than market expectations of -$0.20. Overall, its topline and bottom line performance was still decent.

Moreover, its cash and cash equivalents stayed high at $1.0B. With that, its cash runway increased from my previous coverage. Currently, it still has a cash runway of 32.7 months or two years and eight months. If we match it with its target SSLB release, QS is still very much consistent with its budget. If we add the other operating expenses, its cash runway will decrease but remain high at 26.2 months or two years and two months.

Volkswagen's Popularity Makes QS Even More Promising Today

Despite the mixed views, EVs have remained popular with rising sales in recent years. In a report by CleanTechnica, car sales have already rebounded to pre-pandemic levels. Yet, this was mainly due to EVs. As you can see, the portion of EVs to the total cars sold has increased since 2020.

More interestingly, Volkswagen has maintained its improved demand and popularity since my previous coverage. Last July, it still ranked fifth after selling 53,555 units. It was also its second-best performance for 2025. You can check my previous articles to confirm it. In August, it only ranked seventh with 43,471 units sold. But on a YoY basis, the total number increased by 2.5%. So from January to August or YTD, it sold a total number of 380,596, up by 3.1% and still a bit far higher than BMW and Leapmotor. In only two months, VW sold an additional 96,430 units from my previous coverage.

I would like to reiterate its importance since VW has a huge stake in QuantumScape and a non-exclusive licensing agreement with it. And if you remember, VW increased its funding to the company. So, higher EV sales mean VW can sustain its funding for the development of QS's SSLB technology platform. This can also expand its financial support to QS's other innovations and R&D expenses. With that, QS may be assured of its liquidity without cost-cutting to ensure high-accuracy/quality SSLB technology. In the long run, this can lead to more collaborations and even demand.

Possible Risks

As a speculative stock, the primary risk it may face is its inability to meet its roadmap. Note that the next step is highly crucial and technical, which is the installation of higher-volume cell production equipment with PowerCo. If this is not completed as planned, succeeding steps may be affected. It may also incur additional expenses.

Stubborn inflation and tariffs must also be considered since they may cause extra cost pressures. Retaliation is also possible, which can impact QS's global penetration.

The consolation is that its cash runway remains big enough to cover potential delays and additional expenses. It's fundamentally sound to ensure its development until it reaches the commercial phase.

Valuation

After QS increased by over 40% since my previous coverage, I decided to revisit its valuation. Again, the price trend remains consistent with cash per share or CPS. Using P/CPS, the stock now trades at 36.03x. Yet, it is still lower than the five-year average of 39.22x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price will be $14.90. Somehow, I understand the selloff last October 15 and 22. The current price is already close to our new target. Nonetheless, it is still trading at a reasonable level, so buying QS remains ideal. If we add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will still be higher than the current price at ($14.90 * (1 - 5%)) $14.16.

I also used book value per share. With P/B, the stock only trades at 6.61x versus the average of 9.73x. The higher P/B today shows that the price increased faster than book value but became cheaper than in previous years. As a result, the TP remains high at $20.15. As a speculative stock, I added a 5% risk consideration. Hence, the TP will be ($20.15 * (1 - 5%)) $19.14.

Technicals

QS remains bullish despite the selloff yesterday. The price increases with new highs and lows above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, so the uptrend still holds. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards above the 200-day SMA. With that, market sentiments are still optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI explain the logic behind the recent selloff. The MACD line has already crossed below the signal line, showing that selling volume kept increasing. This is supported by the histogram that is already within negative territory. This means that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Despite this, RSI shows the recent overbuying that led to profit-taking. Now, it's back to the normal range. This can signal that sellers may slow down. It's also near 40, so buyers may enter or re-enter.

Conclusion

QuantumScape Corporation is still in development phases and requires a lot of patience and trust supported by well-founded facts. Yet, its current milestones and financial performance prove that it is right on track and can deliver what we expect from it. Its valuation is also reasonable at this point, so there is still upside potential. Technicals also present new buying opportunities after the recent selloff. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Idaho Strategic Resources: Still Upbeat, Likely As Predicted For Gold
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Idaho Strategic Resources

We believe a continued Buy rating for the NYSE-listed shares of Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. (NYSE:IDR) is justified.

Hereinafter, we may refer to “Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc.” simply as “Idaho” or “IDR.”

In line with our previous article from April 30, 2025, we reiterate this rating for a stock that produces and sells gold, whose price is exploding and whose positive momentum is forecast to continue long-term, and for a stock that is tapping into vast reserves of rare earth elements that, according to IDR's CEO, few in the U.S. currently possess. The administration of President Trump 2.0 appears to be encouraging companies to produce these elements domestically and reduce dependence on China amid ongoing trade tensions between the U.S. and the Asian country.

The market is aware of the importance of these critical elements for industries such as weapons production, the battery industry, electric mobility, and AI-powered data centers. Large U.S. companies, potentially holding large quantities of rare earths to support U.S. growth, could become a true armed wing of US strategy, not only in rare earth negotiations, but potentially also in other sectors where the world's two largest GDP players, the U.S. and China, compete.

Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (CLF), for example, appears to be seizing the opportunity for these raw materials, which will be among the most sought-after commodities on Wall Street in 2025. The Cleveland, Ohio-based U.S. steel producer is showing renewed interest in rare earth programs that signal a strong comeback and has identified two promising sites, one in Michigan and one in Minnesota, as Yahoo Finance reports.

In this context, Yahoo Finance underscored the increasingly central role that rare earths are playing in the trade war between White House and Beijing, writing earlier this week:

Beijing's new restrictions on rare earths and the White House's fresh round of tariff threats highlighted the strategic importance of the critical minerals and the vulnerabilities of not controlling them.

Source: “Rare earths mining is having a crypto moment”, Hamza Shaban, Senior Reporter at Yahoo Finance, Tuesday, October 21, 2025.

Of course, all these movements in the rare earths sector can only reinforce the optimistic sentiment surrounding IDR stock, and for this reason, as well, we reiterate our Buy recommendation for this stock.

About Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc.

The company provides information about itself in its Q2-2025 operating and financial report:

Idaho Strategic Resources is an Idaho-based gold producer which also owns the largest rare earth elements land package in the United States. The Company's business plan was established in anticipation of today's volatile geopolitical and macroeconomic environment. IDR finds itself in a unique position as the only publicly traded company with growing gold production and significant blue-sky potential for rare earth elements exploration and development in one Company.

Source: “Idaho Strategic Reports Record Second Quarter Operating and Financial Performance”, August 7, 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha, originally released by Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. in COEUR D'ALENE, ID via ACCESS Newswire.

Idaho Strategic Resources Stock Performance Since Our Last Article

The positive catalysts of the strong gold price and robust operating results from the Golden Chest Mine in northern Idaho's Murray Gold Belt ("MGB"), along with the company's desire to expand into the rare earths sector—one of the commodities receiving increasing attention on Wall Street—led to a rise in IDR's share price on the NYSE, as we anticipated in our previous article.

Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc., listed on the NYSE under the ticker symbol “IDR,” was up +118.38% at the time of writing, outperforming the S&P 500's change of +22.53%.

Gold Price Outlook

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) was at $4,090.94/ounce as of this writing, up 56.75% year-to-date. An impressive rally that was halted earlier this week by the “biggest daily drops in years,” Yahoo Finance said on Wednesday, Oct. 22, suggesting that experts believe this is temporary due to the strengthening U.S. dollar, while still predicting a rise in the price per ounce in the longer term.

The Yahoo Finance article includes forecasts from Bank of America of a high of $6,000 per ounce by mid-2026 and from Goldman Sachs of $4,900 per troy ounce by the end of 2026. On top of this, about JPMorgan's long-term forecast, Yahoo Finance states the following:

JPMorgan analysts said the yellow metal could hit $6,000 per ounce by 2029.

Source: “Gold, silver tumble in biggest daily drop in years as stunning precious metals rally comes to a halt,” Ines Ferré, Senior Business Reporter at Yahoo Finance, Wednesday, October 22, 2025.

Operating and Financial Figures for the Second Quarter of 2025: What can we expect in the future?

So, the rare earth project is still something that Idaho continues to work on, currently spurred on by pledges of nationalist policies from the U.S. government. As for the gold business, IDR is a small producer in the global industry. They will not be able to bring much more than 12,050 ounces per year to market if we predict 3,010 ounces produced in Q2 2025 over a 12-month period, and the costs are exorbitant.

The costs are objectively exorbitant if we consider that IDR's gold production, with its All-In Sustaining Cost Per Ounce (“AISC”) of $1,980.41 per ounce in Q2 2025, which has also increased consistently by 42.75% year-over-year, is very expensive relative to North American producers, averaging $1,508 per ounce at the end of last year. This estimate is according to Sarah Tomlinson, Director of Mine Supply Metals Focus for the World Gold Council. Sarah Tomlinson attributes the continued rise in AISC to the soaring price of gold per ounce, writing:

Rising prices means marginal, lower grade ore becomes economic. They also lead to higher royalties and mining tax payments as revenues and profits increase. Improving producer margins provide the financial headroom for companies to invest in the recapitalization of assets and progress mine developments, which can lead to higher sustaining capital expenditure.

Source: “Ever upwards for AISC, but distinct regional variations are emerging” on March 7, 2025, on the World Gold Council website, Sarah Tomlinson, Director of Mine Supply Metals Focus.

As one can easily understand, the tide that triggered the sharp rise in the price of gold, “a surge,” as one should aptly call it, is crucial for IDR to be able to report a visible improvement in sales and profits. As the screenshot of the figures for the second quarter of 2025 below shows, it provides strong evidence of that:

Therefore, in the second quarter of 2025, it was possible for IDR a CapEx of $1.7 million invested in its ongoing gold drilling projects – 5,819 meters were completed at Golden Chest, highlighting three distinct zones with multiple high-grade occurrences, as well as at the Murray Gold Belt, the Buckskin and King mines, and at Eastern Star – and also upgraded the New Jersey Mill with a new tailings filter circuit and tailings recirculation to the Golden Chest mine, which effectively began in the second quarter of 2025.

Investments were also made in rare earth exploration on claims encompassing approximately 1,500 acres of various claims within the rare earth element projects of the Company's Mineral Hill, collectively containing over 17% rare earth oxides, and Lemhi Pass, which are leased under a long-term lease. The Earth Element projects are described in more detail in our previous articles on IDR, which our readers may wish to refer to.

John Swallow, Strategic President and CEO of IDR Idaho, commented on the second quarter 2025 operating and financial report:

"At IDR, record quarterly revenue and cash flow brings with it record activity and record investment back into the production side of the business and in gold and rare earth elements exploration.

[…]

The results listed above are impressive by any measure, and the rare earth side of our business advanced nicely during the quarter, from a macro level as well as from boots on the ground. […]."

Source: “Idaho Strategic Reports Record Second Quarter Operating and Financial Performance”, August 7, 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha, originally released by Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. in COEUR D'ALENE, ID via ACCESS Newswire.

But IDR, supported by gold prices, generated $8.3 million operating cash flow ("OCF"), resulting in free cash flow of about $1.8 million or $0.13/share for the June 2025 quarter alone (Free Cash Flow = OCF minus CapEx).

John Swallow, Strategic President and CEO of IDR Idaho, also stated:

“[…] Our balance sheet is strong and this years' re-investment back into the asset base is on track. I am looking forward to the next quarter and a strong finish to 2025."

Source: “Idaho Strategic Reports Record Second Quarter Operating and Financial Performance”, August 7, 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha, originally released by Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. in COEUR D'ALENE, ID via ACCESS Newswire.

What We Can Expect In The Future

Regarding the strong end to 2025 that CEO John Swallow alludes to, we believe that we can expect a clear growth-oriented scenario for IDR going forward: IDR's future gold production is supported by 214,249.8 ounces of gold in total resources (65% M&I and 35% inferred resources) and 49,376.1 ounces of gold in reserves as of December 31, 2024, with a potential mine life of between 4.1 and 11.7 years (or even longer if inferred ounces are developed), based on approximately 12,000 ounces of gold produced per year.

Given the robust, positive prospects for the gold price, as described in the “Gold Price Outlook” section of this analysis, the gold price remains well above a steady increase in AISC/oz, which, due to the factors from Sarah Tomlinson Metals Focus, we reported previously, rise hand in hand with the price of the metal. Regarding AISC margin (AISC/oz): AISC increased by 42.75% year-on-year to $1,980.41/oz in Q2 2025 in line with the rising gold price, but the yellow metal is moving up faster by 57.71% year-on-year to $3,223.38 in Q2 2025, putting the difference between the two on a growing course in favor of a more profitable margin for IDR, which is ultimately good for the company's balance sheet, crucial to its investment plans.

The Financial Condition Is Improving

Idaho Strategic Resources' financial position at the end of the June 2025 quarter was $13.1 million in total cash and Short-Term investments (81% ST investments plus 19% cash and equivalents). This represents an improvement over $8.9 million in total cash and ST investments at the end of 2024. The balance sheet is enhanced by gold sales receivables of $2.64 million (compared to $1.58 million as of December 31, 2024) and inventory of $1.12 million (compared to $0.9 million at the end of 2024). The balance sheet is burdened by total debt of $3.3 million, primarily in the form of long-term and short-term notes payable.

The key financial strength metrics are:

A) A Net cash position (and not a debt position) of $9.8 million.

B) The Interest Coverage Ratio clearly indicates the ability to economically bear the burden of the financing secured by the notes payable, as IDR generated operating income of$2,516,874 in the second quarter of 2025, but recorded little or no interest expense on its notes payable.

C) Altman Z-score of 19.83 ( Seeking Alpha in the “Risk” section); a score above 3.0 indicates a financial position that is within the safe range, and there is no risk of insolvency within a few years.

Valuation

Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc., listed on the NYSE under the symbol “IDR,” was trading at $34.57 per share at the time of this writing, giving it a market capitalization of $501.45 million. IDR stock was trading just above its midpoint of $32.185 per share of the 52-week range of $9.67 to $54.70 per share and below its 20-day moving average (MA cross) (20, 50, 200, MA, 0). This could indicate that the stock price is not trading high, relatively speaking, despite being up 101.8% over the past year. At the same time, however, the MA band continues to signal near-term bullish momentum and a longer-term uptrend.

The RSI at 45.21 is now trending down from the overbought levels of mid-October 2025, marking the beginning of a new, temporarily bearish sentiment towards gold, which officially entered a correction phase on Wednesday, October 22. VanEck saw this as an opportunity to initiate a position in the precious metal, as reported by Seeking Alpha. Given the prominent bullish call to buy gold despite the very high prices that still prevail for the yellow metal, we believe this is further red tape in favor of a thesis calling for the reinstatement of a buy rating for IDR NYSE shares, which owe practically everything to the historic soar in the precious metal price.

The RSI may now signal uncertain behavior from market participants, as 45.21 signals neutrality relative to a range of 30 (oversold/ completely bearish) to 70 (overbought/ completely bullish). Market participants may be uncertain about the future performance of gold and the highly positively correlated IDR stock. But we believe this indecision will only be temporary before gold followers return to a significantly bullish mood and highly value the earnings of IDR stock, as these are tied to the benefits of rising gold prices. Some eloquent voices on Wall Street also believe that the future of the gold market remains bullish, even after this correction phase. These are the views of Tom Essaye, founder of Sevens Report Research, and Michele Schneider, chief strategist at Marketgauge.com, as reported by Yahoo Finance, along with predictions from major U.S. bank analysts, as already outlined in our analysis above.

Finally, the short interest metric of 13.27% at the time of this article could indicate that additional bearish momentum is building, which could further push the market value of NYSE-listed IDR shares down in the short term. Investors may want to wait and see if what this indicator now appears to predict comes to pass, but based on the factors mentioned in this analysis, this stock deserves a Buy rating.

Conclusion

The profitability outlook, a strong driver of U.S. equity market sentiment, justifies a Buy recommendation for NYSE-listed IDR Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. shares, even with rising AISCs, provided the robust, positive gold price outlook keeps the gold price well above AISC/oz. However, based on the gold price estimates of leading Wall Street analysts described in the “Gold Price Outlook” section of this analysis and the lagging AISC figures during the hunt for rising gold prices, IDR shares appear well on track to benefit from the tailwind of improving sentiment driven by profitability progress.

The US government's nationalist policy of encouraging domestic production of rare earths and reducing reliance on Chinese imports of these raw materials in response to some Chinese retaliatory actions in this area will further boost sentiment around IDR stock as the company makes new announcements about its rare earth project.

data.title: Why Pfizer's Headwinds May Already Be In The Price
data.description:
Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) faces several headwinds ranging from patent expirations to COVID product declines and competition from generics. Some of these factors have mostly played out, like the COVID product cliff, while the others are already acknowledged in today's valuations. That is my Buy thesis. My cautious Buy is not rooted in defense of the headwinds - they are real. I argue how the "most likely scenario" is priced in. Fresh entries will therefore not only likely see a sustained dividend, but capital appreciation due to even partial pipeline and acquisition successes.

Acknowledging the Headwinds

Within the 2026-28 window, 5 of Pfizer's top franchises face loss of exclusivity. In my scenario estimates, I have assumed conservative revenue trajectories of the existing products. Eliquis, facing a patent cliff in 2028, goes through an 8% growth (in line with what we saw in Q2 2025) before an 85% sharp drop due to competition and further drops by 5% every year. That is a whopping ~$8.5b revenue loss from the peak.

The Prevnar family, facing an immediate patent cliff in 2026, is also assumed to see revenues drop by 85% followed by sequential annual drops of 5%. This assumes no settlement scenarios that could limit losses. Similarly Ibrance and Xtandi run into cliffs in 2027 and account for ~$5b revenue drop from peak values. Xeljanz is expiring in 2028 but has a relatively lower impact than the other products at stake.

Taken together, over the next 2-3 years, I believe patent cliffs alone could account for ~$19-20b revenue erosion. There are additional patent expiry impacts on "Other Oncology" too, with varying expiries between 2028-2030 (modeled as a 40% decline in year 1 followed by a 10% decline thereafter). "Other Products" also face competitive erosion due to biosimilar or generic entry in the 2027-29 window. I have taken an overall 45% drop in the first year, followed by 10% drops in subsequent years, acknowledging the varied profile of products here. Some products, like adult Prevnar, should see sharper declines, while smaller injectables should hold up better.

There are impacts of slowing Paxlovid revenues, which I have assumed to decay 2% annually from here on (most of the impact is already in, as seen in Q2 2025 revenues of $450m only). My model also extrapolates a 2% decline in seasonal vaccine demand in Comirnaty.

Since my patent cliff scenarios are aggressive, I have not separately modeled for other risks that exist like the Medicare Part D Rebate Clawbacks that reduce net pricing room for key specialty drugs. The extent of impact there is ~$1b and should already be impacting 2025 revenues.

Plugging the Hole

From the existing product basket, only three products stand out as growth possibilities that can offset some of the existing product erosion. Nurtec is currently growing at ~10% and I have assumed a 8% continued CAGR growth on its lower base. Padcev, a high-growth ADC in bladder/multiple myeloma, grew by ~60% YoY in Q2 2025. I have assumed ~25% CAGR growth here. I also see a continued ~25% CAGR growth in the Vyndaqel Family for its growing use in transthyretin amyloidosis.

Put together, the growth of some existing products still shrinks revenues to ~$45b by 2030. These are aggressive topline decline scenarios for expiring drugs factored in, while the assumed growth of existing products is not out of line - an extrapolation or even more moderate growth rates than current growth have been assumed.

The current valuations of ~8x is lower than the last 5 year average valuations of ~10x earnings (and far below the sector median of ~19x). These valuations bake in a ~20% EPS erosion and revenue of ~54.5b by 2030. Those levels of revenues are still ~$10b higher than the base case scenario I have modeled that factors in aggressive patent risks and viable existing portfolio growth.

With a budget for ~$10b-$15b for acquisitions, and including the impacts of Metsera, I can attribute ~$3-4b as inorganic uplift by 2030. So now the question boils down to how much of the $6-7b can be expected from the pipeline - the biggest swing variable that will be watched.

That asking rate translates to a 4-5 major launches of $1b peak revenue potential and 4-5 smaller launches. There are 28 of 108 pipeline products in stage 3 trials. Sasanlimab is already in the registration phase. I am particularly hopeful about the obesity and diabetes space, which I think is Pfizer’s most visible growth option. Despite setbacks in the past, the success of an oral GLP-1 formulation could translate to huge upsides even if Pfizer can capture a modest market share. Around 10 launches over the next 5 years is a decent ask anyway, translating to ~10% of the overall pipeline and a third of the stage 3 trials alone.

Cautious Buy with a Watchlist

The dividends are an integral part of the Pfizer story given the limited valuation upside potential in the base case scenario. If revenues return to current levels due to organic and inorganic growth, there is bound to be a valuation rerating of 20-30% upside towards the historical norms of ~10x earnings in itself. Even ignoring that possibility, a maintained revenue level of ~$55b is good enough for a dividend yield of 2-3% on a cost basis.

The way I derive the dividend yield downside is to assume a ~25% operating margin and ~30% interest and taxes. For a $55b revenue, the cash flow (without accounting for capex and working capital) works out to be ~$9.5b, just about enough to account for the dividend payouts (5.7b shares outstanding *$0.42 dividend per quarter). Assuming 5-10% of revenue flowing into capex and working capital, I still see room for 2-3% dividend yields, down from the 6-7% levels today.

All in all, the thesis for Pfizer has very limited asks for a Buy to work out. It expects patent cliffs to be aggressive from day 1. It expects the growing products to not ramp up extraordinarily. It expects a ~10% conversion of a pipeline with significant stage 3 presence. It also assumes the worst case scenario for the dividends (no financial engineering or cost optimizations or working capital tweaks, etc.). Despite all the conservative assumptions, the current share price expectations are met, and a still credible dividend payout looks possible, in my view.

I would buy Pfizer at the current levels and keep a watch on how it negotiates the patent cliffs - any settlements or slower than expected revenue erosion could itself be valuation-supportive, and that is very much a likely scenario. Then I will also watch out for pipeline progress, of course, and acquisitions. I see decent chances of double digit total returns in Pfizer for patient investors - partially through the dividends and the rest through the decent optionality of valuation expansion back to recent historical averages.

data.title: Lam Research: A Semiconductor Sector Poster Child
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by, any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice, nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Yes, We Are All Doomed. But Probably Not Today.

The semiconductor sector has seen its stocks and ETFs run up so far and so fast from the post-Liberation Day lows that it’s easy for the casual observer to decide, well, that’s it, all done here, time to take the money and run. And the casual observer may be right. Stock charts are extended, valuations are high, and at some point you would expect the sector to be a source of funds as large investors cash in their gains to then rotate to another less popular sector (software is a good candidate for that, but that’s a story for another day).

Before we get to Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX) itself, let’s just look at the sector overall. I like to use the ETF SOXX to do so - it’s fairly well balanced in its holdings of the top sector names and isn’t overweight NVDA in the way that the also-popular sector ETF SMH is.

Here’s how SOXX looks right now, starting from the April lows.

The factual parts of this chart are:

  • A textbook initial move up from the lows, quickly retraced for a higher low, then a big move up (that’s the Waves 1, 2, and 3; Wave 3 so far having reached the 3.618 Fibonacci extension of Wave 1 placed at the Wave 2 low, which is another way of saying That’s A Big Ole Run Up). Bullish.
  • Price has held over the 21-day exponential moving average solidly since early Q2, with only a few downside breaches all of which were quickly resolved to the upside. Note that on high stress days like 10 October, support held firm at that 21-day EMA (it’s the green line on the chart). Bullish.
  • Price cannot yet push up and over $295 and turn it into support. Potentially bearish, for if there are no buyers in size over $295, people will start to take gains, slowly at first and then more quickly.

The opinion parts of this chart are:

  • I personally like to project downside starting right away if a Wave 3 or a Wave 5 is at extended levels. Not because I think a stock or ETF is necessarily going to dump, but because I want to be prepared if it does. The Wave 4 here, which shows a potential drop to $243 (the .382 Fibonacci retracement of the Wave 3, a fairly shallow correction usually), is just that - psychological preparation. Until SOXX starts selling off, it isn’t selling off.
  • The final Wave 5 in this cycle assumes a correction which bottoms out at $243 and then puts in a typical final bull move up before a more substantial correction.

Now opinion being opinion, you can ignore all the opinion. Could be right, could be wrong, I have no more idea than you whether that’s how the SOXX plays out. It’s just one possibility.

But what you can’t ignore is price not breaking up and over $295, for that is a warning sign. If SOXX starts dumping so too with LRCX and everything else in semiconductor, yes including NVDA. So you need to know this if you hold semiconductor stocks or are thinking of doing so. Equally if price does move up and over $295 and turns it into support, there’s probably another leg up coming for all stocks in the sector.

SOXX $295. That’s your line in the sand. Sometimes securities analysis isn’t as difficult as everyone tells you it is.

Let’s take a look at Lam Research (LRCX) fundamentals.

LRCX Fundamentals

Remarkably strong is how I would describe the numbers here. Semiconductor continues to be the center of the universe as regards economic expansion, capex projects and geopolitics, and that is to the benefit of equipment providers like Lam Research. Unit growth in semiconductor, and in particular increasing function density and reducing feature size, are the unit volume drivers of Lam revenues; the company’s quasi-monopolistic market positions are the price (and therefore margin) drivers. Semiconductor capital equipment is a highly consolidated industry, and if you own the leaders in each of the major categories (LRCX, Applied Materials (AMAT), ASML (ASML), etc.) you can do very well during these cyclical upswings.

A generalized but ill-defined worry hanging over the semiconductor sector is “but what if the US and China decouple,” and I would say that this is a double-edged sword. Yes, there will be major disruption, but the No. 1 loser from that is likely TSMC (TSM); all US-based fabs will need to build out quickly and that means pricing power for companies like LRCX. Investing through such a dislocation in the world order will be challenging no matter what, but it will just require the usual discipline focused on price not narrative, volume not fundamentals. It doesn’t mean it’s all over for chips.

There's nothing not to like in those numbers. Revenue growth is strong, margins are high at the gross, EBITDA and cash flow levels; and the balance sheet with $2.2bn of net cash is safe as houses. The guide for fiscal Q2 (calendar Q4; the company’s financial year ends June) is for a drop in growth, but you can see from the last two December quarters that it's not a seasonally good month for growth. If the company hits the guide, TTM growth will remain at 26%, the highest level for some years.

Lam Research Valuation Analysis

Well, if you asked your grandparents, they would tell you that paying 28x trailing-twelve-month unlevered pretax free cash flow for anything at all is probably nuts. But this isn’t your grandparents’ market. In this market we’re in, the actual market in front of us as opposed to the market that might exist in Corporate Finance 101, this isn’t a bad price for the stock, in my view.

Lam Research Stock Chart

Let’s take a look at how LRCX itself is doing. It won’t surprise you to know that it looks a lot like SOXX.

With a stock that has run up this much - a 5.618 Wave 3 is a huge move - you have to have some caution. Another reason to have some concern is that the volume x price profile - that’s the gray horizontal bars on the chart showing volume of stock traded at any given price - is starting to pick up. I think that may be the beginning of institutional distribution, ie. profit taking. But we don’t have to guess. My rule of thumb for stocks like this is - watch the short-term moving averages. If LRCX is up and over the eight-day SMA (red line) and the 21-day EMA (green line), all good, nothing to worry about. A couple of daily closes below the eight-day but above the 21-day, have your antennae up but no need to freak out. Two daily closes below the 21-day, time to pack up and leave in my view. You can always get back in if it recaptures that moving average.

Rating

A cautious and disengaged investor would likely take profits here. But if you don’t mind watching price closely, I’d say this warrants a Hold rating until such time as there are two daily closes below that 21-day moving average, in which case I personally would take my money and run. You, as always, will do you.

Alex King, CEO, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 24 October 2025.

data.title: Snowflake: Strong Momentum And A Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Snowflake's (NYSE:SNOW) stock has rebounded in recent months on the back of low expectations and a meaningful growth reacceleration. A lack of direct exposure to AI tailwinds and uncertainty around the impact of Apache Iceberg on Snowflake's storage revenue contributed to these low expectations.

I previously suggested that second-half expectations could be difficult to fulfill. Snowflake's stock is up close to 60% since then, though, with the demand environment seemingly solidifying and newer products supporting growth.

Snowflake's valuation is still reasonable for longer-term investors, although I would be cautious about extrapolating recent strength. As a result, I believe that Snowflake's stock will perform well over the longer term, albeit with periods of potentially elevated volatility.

Market Conditions

AI spending continues to dominate the IT market, with near-term growth expectations generally moving higher. The impact of this on software infrastructure companies has so far been fairly limited outside the cloud hyperscalers, though. AWS's recent growth could be considered disappointing given the AI backdrop. This is potentially problematic for Snowflake, as AWS still constitutes the majority of deployments. Azure is an emerging area of strength, though, with Snowflake's Azure business growing 40% YoY in the most recent quarter. This is interesting given that Microsoft (MSFT) has its own data platform (Fabric), which is targeted at analytics use cases. Fabric is tightly integrated into Microsoft's ecosystem and offers users a range of AI-powered tools. Fabric is likely to be viewed as a strategic area for Microsoft given the growing importance of AI agents. While the product serves over 21,000 paid customers, up 80% year over year, it is not clear that it is negatively impacting Snowflake.

The number of Snowflake job openings has fallen sharply in recent months, which could be suggestive of weaker demand, although it could also be due to seasonality and an increased focus on costs. Snowflake has also been aggressively hiring sales and marketing personnel in recent quarters, some of which relates to rebuilding the sales force after a period of extensive performance management in the prior year.

The number of job openings mentioning Snowflake in the job requirements has picked up sharply, which points towards improved demand. While this hasn't been reflected in improved net customer additions yet, Snowflake's revenue growth rate has already strengthened.

While it seems reasonable to expect software revenue growth to accelerate at some point, I would argue that Snowflake's direct exposure to AI is still fairly limited at the moment. Databricks has proven to be much more of an AI beneficiary so far, and recently raised to over a 100 billion valuation, up from a $62 billion valuation in late 2024. There remains a large opportunity that Snowflake is only just beginning to capitalize on, though. As a result, the company expects its growth to remain strong for many years to come.

Snowflake Business Updates

The adoption of open data formats is an important trend currently, due to the potential impact on Snowflake's revenue and its competitive positioning. Snowflake has seen strong adoption of open formats, with over 1,200 Snowflake accounts now using Iceberg.

Snowflake recently announced the planned acquisition of Crunchy Data for an estimated $250 million. Crunchy Data provides customers with tools to build Postgres solutions.
